title: Problem 1.1 (Erdős and Hajnal)
statement_text: Is it true that for every $r\in\mathbb{N}$ there exists a number $F(r)$ such that every graph of chromatic number at least $F(r)$ contains $r$ edge-disjoint cycles on the same vertex set?
context_text: Erdős and Hajnal raised this problem in 1992 [7, Problem 6]; it was repeated by Erdős in 1997 and included in Chung and Graham's 1998 book on Erdős's unsolved problems. It asks whether sufficiently large chromatic number forces a rich cycle structure, motivated by the observation that large chromatic number implies many edges.
paper_contribution: Gives a strong negative answer by constructing $n$-vertex graphs with fractional chromatic number $\Omega\!\left(\frac{\log\log n}{\log\log\log n}\right)$ that contain no $4$-regular subgraph, thereby showing that no such number $F(r)$ exists for $r\geq 2$.

title: Conjecture 3.1 (Harris)
statement_text: There exists an absolute constant $K>0$ such that for every sufficiently large $d\in\mathbb{N}$, every triangle-free $d$-degenerate graph $G$ satisfies $\chi_{f}(G)\leq K\frac{d}{\log d}$.
context_text: This conjecture of Harris [9] concerns the fractional chromatic number of triangle-free degenerate graphs and has received significant attention in recent years. The paper invokes it to show that the $\Omega\!\left(\frac{\log\log n}{\log\log\log n}\right)$ lower bound on the fractional chromatic number in the main result (Theorem 1.2) cannot be improved.
paper_contribution: Shows, via a subsampling argument (Proposition 3.2), that assuming Harris's conjecture the bound on the fractional chromatic number in Theorem 1.2 is tight up to constant factors.
